A young weaver named Elara, tired of the perpetual gloom, decided to catch the light of the fireflies. For years, she spun a thread so fine it was invisible to the naked eye, trapping thousands of the glowing insects within a massive, shimmering net. One evening, she climbed the tallest peak, dragging her net behind her. With a mighty heave, she cast the net upwards, aiming to pin it against the heavy velvet of the Night.

The net stuck, but the fireflies struggled, tearing tiny holes in the fabric of the dark. Through these pinpricks, the brilliant light of the universe beyond leaked through. The largest tear became the Moon, pouring silver onto the lands below, while the smaller rips became the stars.

Exhausted but triumphant, Elara realized the darkness was not gone, but it was no longer absolute. The Night Sky remained, but now it was a canvas for light, guiding travelers home and reminding the world that even the thickest darkness can be pierced.

She squeezed the juice of the sun-berries into the rivers, turning them a deep, flowing teal. She took the blush from a shy mountain spirit to paint the dawn pink and the dusk purple.
